name: Modern Neovim Config with lazy.nvim
description: Set up a modern Neovim configuration from scratch using lazy.nvim as the plugin manager with a clean directory structure.

steps.title: Back up existing config and create directory structure
steps.description: Save any existing Neovim config and set up the standard directory layout.
steps.tip: Neovim reads init.lua from ~/.config/nvim/ on Linux/macOS or ~/AppData/Local/nvim/ on Windows.

steps.title: Bootstrap lazy.nvim in init.lua
steps.description: Create the init.lua with the lazy.nvim bootstrap code that auto-installs the plugin manager.
steps.command:
cat > ~/.config/nvim/init.lua << 'EOF'
-- Bootstrap lazy.nvim
local lazypath = vim.fn.stdpath("data") .. "/lazy/lazy.nvim"
if not vim.loop.fs_stat(lazypath) then
  vim.fn.system({ "git", "clone", "--filter=blob:none",
    "https://github.com/folke/lazy.nvim.git", "--branch=stable", lazypath })
end
vim.opt.rtp:prepend(lazypath)

-- Leader key (must be before lazy)
vim.g.mapleader = " "
vim.g.maplocalleader = " "

-- Core options
vim.opt.number = true
vim.opt.relativenumber = true
vim.opt.tabstop = 4
vim.opt.shiftwidth = 4
vim.opt.expandtab = true
vim.opt.smartindent = true
vim.opt.termguicolors = true
vim.opt.signcolumn = "yes"
vim.opt.clipboard = "unnamedplus"

-- Load plugins
require("lazy").setup("plugins")
EOF

steps.title: Add a starter plugin spec
steps.description: Create a plugin spec file that installs a colorscheme and essential plugins.
steps.command:
cat > ~/.config/nvim/lua/plugins/init.lua << 'EOF'
return {
  { "catppuccin/nvim", name = "catppuccin", priority = 1000,
    config = function() vim.cmd.colorscheme("catppuccin") end },
  { "nvim-treesitter/nvim-treesitter", build = ":TSUpdate" },
  { "nvim-telescope/telescope.nvim", dependencies = { "nvim-lua/plenary.nvim" } },
}
EOF

steps.title: Launch Neovim and install plugins
steps.description: Open Neovim to trigger lazy.nvim's automatic plugin installation.
steps.tip: Run :Lazy inside Neovim to see the plugin manager UI, update plugins, and check health.

steps.title: Verify the setup
steps.description: Check that Neovim starts cleanly with no errors.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Neovim Config Setup with lazy.nvim
set -euo pipefail

NVIM_DIR="${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-$HOME/.config}/nvim"

echo "=== Setting Up Neovim Config ==="

# Backup existing config
if [ -d "$NVIM_DIR" ]; then
  echo "Backing up existing config to $NVIM_DIR.bak"
  mv "$NVIM_DIR" "$NVIM_DIR.bak"
fi

mkdir -p "$NVIM_DIR/lua/plugins"

echo "Created directory structure:"
find "$NVIM_DIR" -type d

echo ""
echo "=== Next Steps ==="
echo "1. Create $NVIM_DIR/init.lua with lazy.nvim bootstrap"
echo "2. Add plugin specs in $NVIM_DIR/lua/plugins/"
echo "3. Run 'nvim' to auto-install plugins"
echo ""
echo "=== Useful Commands ==="
echo ":Lazy          Open plugin manager"
echo ":Lazy sync     Install/update plugins"
echo ":Lazy health   Check plugin status"
echo ":checkhealth   Run Neovim health checks"

faq.question: What is the difference between lazy.nvim and packer.nvim?
faq.answer: lazy.nvim is the successor to packer. It offers lazy-loading by default, a modern UI, automatic dependency resolution, and lockfile support. packer is no longer maintained.

faq.question: Where does Neovim store plugin data?
faq.answer: Plugins are stored in ~/.local/share/nvim/lazy/ on Linux/macOS. The lazy-lock.json file in your config directory pins plugin versions for reproducible setups.

faq.question: How do I migrate from an existing Vim config?
faq.answer: Start fresh with init.lua rather than converting vimrc. Neovim's Lua API is more powerful. You can still source a .vim file with vim.cmd('source ~/.vimrc') during migration.
